March 30, 1979 
Thank you for your letter of March 21, 1979 regarding 
the campaign stateTIent of Gay W. Harrington of the Inglewood 
Judicial District. 
Government Code Section 8l005(e) specifies in part 
that, "Reports and statements of candidates for and persons 
holding any elective office ... to be voted upon in not 
more than one county" file an original and one copy with 
the county clerk. 
Therefore, campaign statements for Gay W. Harrington 
are available for public review at the Campaign Reporting 
Unit of the Registrar-Recorder of Los An~eles. 
That 
address is 808 North Spring Street, Los Angeles. 
If our office can be of further assistance, please 
do not hesitate to contact us.

Ms. Stella Wallace 
Registrar-Recorder of 
Los Angeles County 
Campaign Reporting Unit 
808 North Spring Street 
Los Angeles, California 90012 
Dear Ms. Wallace: 
March 12, 1979 
This letter is to confirm our telephone conversation of 
March 7, 1979. 
The question you posed was whether or not a penalty that 
has been assessed a committee included the date of the 
postmark of that campaign statement that was mailed, or 
the date that your office actually received the campaign 
statement? 
I advised that the penalty should include and end on the 
date of the postmark of the mailed campaign statement. 
The 
same principal that is used for campaign statements which 
are mailed must be postmarked by no later than midnight of 
the date on which the campaign statement is due is 
applicable. 
This advice has been confirmed by Barbara Campbell 
of our Legal Division in Sacramento. 
I have enclosed a copy 
of a factsheet which the Commission has developed for the 
filing of campaign statements by mail (Government Code 
Section 81007) for your information. 
If our office can be of further assistance, please 
do not hesitate to call. 
HPA:jg 
Enclosure: 
Factsheet 7/78 
SACRAMENTO (916) 322·5660

sections.question_synthetic: This document contains two separate advice letters: (1) regarding where campaign statements for a judicial candidate should be filed and reviewed, and (2) clarifying whether penalty assessment dates should be based on postmark date or receipt date for mailed campaign statements.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Campaign statements for single-county candidates must be filed with the county clerk and are available for public review at the county registrar's office. For penalty assessment purposes, the relevant date is the postmark date of the mailed campaign statement, not the date the office received it, consistent with the filing deadline rule requiring postmarks by midnight of the due date.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two separate advice letters without formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION section headers. Text is partially degraded/faint in places. One letter references that this advice was later reversed by Opinion No. 79-002. First letter addresses Ms. Kaczmar's inquiry; second addresses Ms. Wallace's telephone inquiry. Confidence reduced due to lack of formal section headers and partial text degradation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: This document contains two separate advice letters: (1) regarding where campaign statements for a judicial candidate should be filed and reviewed, and (2) clarifying whether penalty assessment dates should be based on postmark date or receipt date for mailed campaign statements.

CONCLUSION: Campaign statements for single-county candidates must be filed with the county clerk and are available for public review at the county registrar's office. For penalty assessment purposes, the relevant date is the postmark date of the mailed campaign statement, not the date the office received it, consistent with the filing deadline rule requiring postmarks by midnight of the due date.

embedding.summary: Two FPPC advice letters from March 1979 addressing campaign statement filing requirements for a judicial candidate and clarifying the proper date to use for penalty assessment on late-filed campaign statements.
